content.text: Visual Studio Code is the most widely used editor for Angular projects. It provides TypeScript-aware IntelliSense, integrated debugging, a built-in terminal for running the Angular CLI, and a rich extension ecosystem that brings Angular-specific tooling directly into the editor.

attrs.alt: VS Code layout for Angular: Explorer, Editor, Terminal, Extensions

content.text: The diagram above shows the four main zones you work with: the Explorer sidebar for navigating your project tree, the editor area with syntax-highlighted TypeScript files, the integrated terminal for running Angular CLI commands, and the extensions panel where Angular Language Service and other tools are installed.
The following extensions are the standard toolkit for Angular development in VS Code:
IntelliSense, type-checking, and go-to-definition inside HTML templates
Inline lint errors from your project's ESLint config (replaces TSLint)
Auto-formats TypeScript, HTML, SCSS on save
Inline git blame, history, and diff annotations
Inline error/warning text next to the offending line
Class autocomplete — only needed when using Tailwind
Shorthand snippets: a-component, a-service, a-pipe, etc.
Install from the command line to share with your team:

code --install-extension Angular.ng-template
code --install-extension dbaeumer.vscode-eslint
code --install-extension esbenp.prettier-vscode
code --install-extension eamodio.gitlens
code --install-extension EditorConfig.EditorConfig
code --install-extension usernamehw.errorlens
Commit a .vscode/settings.json file to enforce consistent editor behaviour for every contributor:
: tells VS Code to use the project's TypeScript version instead of VS Code's built-in one — important when you need TS 5.x features.
: auto-applies ESLint fixes on save so you never commit lint errors.
(Angular's build cache) from the Explorer without deleting it.
VS Code shows a prompt to install recommended extensions when a new contributor opens the workspace:
The identifiers are the extension IDs visible in the Extensions Marketplace. New team members see a notification and can install all listed extensions in one click.
Open file by name — fastest way to jump between component files
Command Palette — run any VS Code or extension command
Toggle sidebar — reclaim screen space during editing
Toggle integrated terminal — run ng commands without leaving the editor
Go to Definition — works inside Angular templates with Language Service
Find All References — see every place a component, service, or symbol is used
Rename Symbol — refactor across the whole project at once
Search across the workspace — useful for finding usages in templates
Quick Fix — apply ESLint fix or import missing symbol
VS Code can launch Chrome (or Edge) with a debugger attached so you can set breakpoints directly in TypeScript source files:
Start the dev server in one terminal:
Open the Run and Debug panel (Cmd+Shift+D / Ctrl+Shift+D).
from the dropdown and press F5.
VS Code opens Chrome with breakpoints live — click a line number in any .ts file to set one.
The Angular Snippets extension (John Papa) provides shorthand triggers in TypeScript and HTML files. Type the prefix and press Tab:
Full @Component class scaffold with selector, templateUrl, styleUrls
@Injectable service scaffold with constructor and optional HttpClient
NgModule scaffold with declarations, imports, exports
Route object with path, component, data
You can also create project-specific snippets via Command Palette → "Snippets: Configure User Snippets" → "New Global Snippets file":
With Angular Language Service installed, HTML template files gain the same IntelliSense features as TypeScript files:
Autocomplete for component inputs, outputs, and template variables.
input is flagged as an error.
jumps to the method in the component class.
Rename refactoring: renaming a method in the class automatically updates its uses in the template.
control-flow equivalents) all have syntax validation.

These features only work when the Language Service can find the component class associated with the template file, which requires a standard Angular project structure (matching file names, decorators with templateUrl pointing to the file).
For Angular monorepos or projects that span multiple directories, create a .code-workspace file:

Each folder shows as a top-level root in Explorer. Tasks and launch configs can reference any root by its name. For Nx or Angular CLI workspaces you would typically keep a single root pointing at the monorepo root instead.
An .editorconfig file at the project root is read by the EditorConfig extension and overrides user settings for indentation, line endings, and trailing whitespace:

# .editorconfig
root = true

[*]
indent_style = space
indent_size = 2
end_of_line = lf
charset = utf-8
trim_trailing_whitespace = true
insert_final_newline = true

[*.md]
trim_trailing_whitespace = false
This is particularly important on teams mixing macOS, Linux, and Windows: it prevents Windows CRLF line endings from appearing in commits and polluting diffs.
Running Angular CLI Tasks from VS Code
The built-in terminal (Ctrl+`) is the primary way to invoke the Angular CLI. You can also define VS Code Tasks for commonly used commands:
(build) or via Command Palette → "Tasks: Run Task". The
lets VS Code know when the server has fully started.
Language Service extension not active or tsconfig missing
Check Extensions panel; ensure tsconfig.json is at the project root
Editor uses its bundled TypeScript, not the project's
Terminal opens with wrong Node version
VS Code spawns shell before nvm init
Source maps not generated or misaligned
Prettier enforces consistent code style across .ts, .html, and .scss files. Create a .prettierrc file at the project root:
override is important — it formats Angular template syntax correctly, preserving structural directives like
control-flow blocks rather than mangling them with the default HTML parser.
Add a .prettierignore file to skip generated and vendor files:
The Problems panel (Cmd+Shift+M / Ctrl+Shift+M) aggregates TypeScript errors, ESLint warnings, and other diagnostics in one place. For Angular projects:
— reported live by the language server as you type. Red squiggles in the editor, red circle in the Problems tab.
— appear after save (or continuously if you have
— reported by Angular Language Service. For example, passing a
terminal, build errors also surface in Problems via the TypeScript problem matcher.
Click any entry in Problems to jump to the exact file and line. Use
to cycle through errors without touching the mouse.
VS Code's built-in Git panel (Ctrl+Shift+G) covers the most common daily Git operations without leaving the editor:
Click + next to file in Source Control panel
Type message in text box, click ✔ or Ctrl+Enter
Click branch name in status bar
Click cloud icon in status bar or use ... menu
Accept Current / Incoming / Both buttons in editor

The GitLens extension enhances this with inline blame annotations, a rich timeline view, commit search, and interactive rebase support — making it practical to manage complex branch histories entirely from within VS Code.
VS Code provides several built-in refactoring operations for TypeScript that work correctly in Angular component files:
(Cmd+Shift+R / Ctrl+Shift+R): select a block of code and extract it into a named method.
: assign an expression to a new const or let.
: move a class, interface, or function to a separate .ts file and update all imports automatically.
(Shift+Alt+O): removes unused imports and sorts the remaining ones alphabetically.

Access all refactoring options from the Quick Fix menu (Cmd+. / Ctrl+.) or by right-clicking and choosing "Refactor...". For safe renames that span .ts and .html template files, always use F2 (Rename Symbol) — a simple text search-and-replace will miss template bindings.
Beyond the standard Chrome debugger launch config, these techniques help debug Angular-specific issues:
from the DevTools console to manually trigger change detection.
: install the Angular DevTools Chrome extension for a component tree inspector and profiler. It works alongside VS Code's debugger.
returns the live component instance so you can inspect its state.
: VS Code maps breakpoints through TypeScript and Webpack source maps to the running bundle. If breakpoints are offset, check that
is in tsconfig.json and that build optimization is disabled during development.
Angular 12+ projects use ESLint via the @angular-eslint schematic. If your project was migrated from TSLint or generated with ESLint from the start, the config lives in .eslintrc.json:
The ESLint VS Code extension reads this file automatically. Errors appear inline in the editor and in the Problems panel. Run
from the terminal to check the whole project at once.

VS Code becomes a purpose-built Angular IDE when configured correctly. The three highest-impact steps are: install Angular Language Service for template IntelliSense, commit .vscode/settings.json and .vscode/extensions.json to align the whole team, and configure launch.json for breakpoint debugging. From there, keyboard shortcuts, snippets, and EditorConfig keep your workflow fast and consistent.
